en.extract:
The orange-breasted forest robin (Stiphrornis erythrothorax) is a species of bird mainly found throughout the African tropical rainforest. It has a total length of around 12 cm (4+3⁄4 in), has dark upperparts, and a throat and chest that, depending on the subspecies, is yellow-orange or deep orange.


Description
It has a total length of around 12 cm (4+3⁄4 in), has dark upperparts, and a throat and chest that, depending on the exact subspecies, is yellow-orange or deep orange.


Taxonomy
The orange-breasted forest robin was formally described in 1855 by the German ornithologist Gustav Hartlaub under the current binomial name Stiphrornis erythrothorax. The specimen had been collected by the Dutch zoologist and colonial administrator Hendrik Severinus Pel at Dabocrom, Ghana. The specific epithet combines the Ancient Greek ερυθρος/eruthros meaning "red" with θωραξ/thōrax meaning "breastplate".
The initial split into multiple species within this genus is based on a review from 1999 where it, based on the phylogenetic species concept, was argued that all then recognized taxa should be considered monotypic species. Of these, S. gabonensis and S. xanthogaster were formerly considered subspecies of S. erythrothorax, whereas S. sanghensis was described as an entirely new species. The split was not followed in Handbook of the Birds of the World, where it was described as "perhaps premature". Comparably, the BirdLife Taxonomic Working Group (and consequently IUCN) recommended not following the split, as differences in plumages are relatively small, genetic sampling considered incomplete, and evidence for intergradation or parapatry is lacking. Another species from this complex, S. pyrrholaemus, was described as a new species in 2008. Based on mtDNA, it is placed within S. erythrothorax sensu lato, and consequently is only a species (rather than a subspecies of S. erythrothorax) if at least some of the taxonomy recommended in 1999 is followed. The genetic divergence between S. pyrrholaemus and other members of the genus is comparable to that between some other closely related species.
Three additional taxa in the forest robin complex were described (as species) in 2016: Stiphrornis (erythrothorax) dahomeyensis (Dahomey forest robin), S. (e.) inexpectatus (Ghana forest robin), and S. (e.) rudderi (Rudder's forest robin). These three taxa are nested within S. erythrothorax sensu lato; however, using the phylogenetic species concept, the study recommended the treatment of all eight forest robin taxa as distinct species.
Clements recognizes three species. the olive-backed forest robin, Stiphrornis pyrrholaemus, the orange-breasted forest robin, Stiphrornis erythrothorax, and the yellow-breasted forest robin, Stiphrornis mabirae.


Subspecies
Four subspecies are recognised:

S. e. erythrothorax Hartlaub, 1855 – Sierra Leone to southwest Ghana
S. e. gabonensis Sharpe, 1883 – Ghana east to central Cameroon and north Gabon; Bioko (Gulf of Guinea)
S. e. dahomeyensis Voelker, Tobler, Prestridge, Duijm, Groenenberg, Martin, AD, Nieman & Roselaar & Huntley, 2017 – south Benin, southeast Ghana
S. e. inexpectatus Voelker, Tobler, Prestridge, Duijm, Groenenberg, Martin, AD, Nieman & Roselaar & Huntley, 2017 – southwest Ghana


Notes


References

The '''orange-breasted forest robin''' ('''''Stiphrornis erythrothorax''''') is a species of bird mainly found throughout the [[Tropical rainforest|African tropical rainforest]]. It has a total length of around {{convert|12|cm|in|frac=4|abbr=on}}, has dark upperparts, and a throat and chest that, depending on the subspecies, is yellow-orange or deep orange.<ref name=HBW>{{ cite book | last=Collar | first=N.J. | year=2005 | chapter=Forest Robin (''Stiphrornis erythrothorax'') | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Christie | editor3-first=D.A. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=10: Cuckoo-shrikes to Thrushes | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-72-6 | pages=730–731 | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0010unse/page/730/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

==Description==
It has a total length of around {{convert|12|cm|in|frac=4|abbr=on}}, has dark upperparts, and a throat and chest that, depending on the exact subspecies, is yellow-orange or deep orange.<ref name=HBW/>

==Taxonomy==
The orange-breasted forest robin was [[species description|formally described]] in 1855 by the German ornithologist [[Gustav Hartlaub]] under the current [[binomial name]] ''Stiphrornis erythrothorax''. The specimen had been collected by the Dutch zoologist and colonial administrator [[Hendrik Severinus Pel]] at Dabocrom, Ghana.{{efn|The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]], "Dabocrom", is believed to have been near the Ghanese coast between [[Abidjan]] and [[Accra]] at {{coord|4|51|N|1|56|W}}.<ref name=voelker/>}}<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Hartlaub | first=Gustav | author-link=Gustav Hartlaub | date=1855 | title=Beschreibung einiger neuen, von Herrn H. S. Pel, holländischem Residenten an der Goldküste, daselbst gesammelten Vögelarten  | journal=Journal für Ornithologie | language=German | volume=3 | issue=17 | pages=353–361 [355] | doi=10.1007/BF02009690 | bibcode=1855JOrn....3..353H | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13947513 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Paynter | editor2-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1964 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=10 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | pages=34–35 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14486223 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ερυθρος/''eruthros'' meaning "red" with θωραξ/''thōrax'' meaning "breastplate".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=erythrothorax | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=erythrothorax | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=3 April 2025 }}</ref>

The initial split into multiple species within this genus is based on a review from 1999 where it, based on the [[phylogenetic species concept]], was argued that all then recognized [[taxa]] should be considered [[monotypic]] species.<ref name=several>{{ cite journal | last1=Beresford | first1=P. | last2=Cracraft | first2=J. | author2-link=Joel Cracraft| date=1999 | title=Speciation in African forest robins (''Stiphrornis''): species limits, phylogenetic relationships, and molecular biogeography | journal=American Museum Novitates | issue=3270 | pages=1–22 | hdl=2246/3048 | url=http://hdl.handle.net/2246/3048 }}</ref> Of these, ''S. gabonensis'' and ''S. xanthogaster'' were formerly considered subspecies of ''S. erythrothorax'', whereas ''S. sanghensis'' was described as an entirely new species.<ref name=several/> The split was not followed in [[Handbook of the Birds of the World]], where it was described as ''"perhaps premature"''.<ref name=HBW/> Comparably, the [[BirdLife International|BirdLife]] Taxonomic Working Group (and consequently [[IUCN]]) recommended not following the split, as differences in [[plumage]]s are relatively small, genetic sampling considered incomplete, and evidence for [[intergradation]] or [[parapatry]] is lacking.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> Another species from this [[species complex|complex]], ''S. pyrrholaemus'', was [[Bird species new to science described in the 2000s|described as a new species]] in 2008. Based on [[mtDNA]], it is placed within ''S. erythrothorax [[sensu lato]]'', and consequently is only a species (rather than a subspecies of ''S. erythrothorax'') if at least some of the [[Taxonomy (biology)|taxonomy]] recommended in 1999 is followed.<ref name=pyrrholaemus>{{cite journal|last1=Schmidt|first1=B.|last2=Foster|first2=J.|last3=Angehr|first3=G.|last4=Durrant|first4=K.|last5=Fleischer|first5=R.|title=A new species of African Forest Robin from Gabon (Passeriformes: Muscicapidae: ''Stiphrornis'')|journal=Zootaxa|date=2008|volume=1850|pages=27–42|doi=10.11646/zootaxa.1850.1.2|url=https://repository.si.edu/bitstream/handle/10088/7476/bks5.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y|doi-access=free}}</ref> The [[genetic divergence]] between ''S. pyrrholaemus'' and other members of the genus is comparable to that between some other closely related species.<ref name=pyrrholaemus/>

Three additional taxa in the forest robin complex were described (as species) in 2016: ''Stiphrornis'' (''erythrothorax'') ''dahomeyensis'' (Dahomey forest robin), ''S.'' (''e.'') ''inexpectatus'' (Ghana forest robin), and ''S.'' (''e.'') ''rudderi'' (Rudder's forest robin). These three taxa are nested within ''S. erythrothorax sensu lato''; however, using the phylogenetic species concept, the study recommended the treatment of all eight forest robin taxa as distinct species.<ref name=voelker>{{cite journal|last1=Voelker|first1=G.|last2=Tobler|first2=M.|last3=Prestridge|first3=H. L.|last4=Duijm|first4=E.|last5=Groenenberg|first5=D.|last6=Hutchinson|first6=M. R.|last7=Martin|first7=A. D.|last8=Nieman|first8=A.|last9=Roselaar|first9=C. S.|last10=Huntley|first10=J. W.|title=Three new species of ''Stiphrornis'' (Aves: Muscicapidae) from the Afro-tropics, with a molecular phylogenetic assessment of the genus|journal=Systematics and Biodiversity|date=2016|volume=15|issue=2|pages=87–104|doi=10.1080/14772000.2016.1226978|s2cid=89146340}}</ref>

Clements recognizes three species. the olive-backed forest robin,'' Stiphrornis pyrrholaemus'', the orange-breasted forest robin, ''Stiphrornis erythrothorax'', and the yellow-breasted forest robin, ''Stiphrornis mabirae''.

===Subspecies===
Four [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Chats, Old World flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/chats/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=3 April 2025}}</ref>

* ''S. e. erythrothorax'' [[Gustav Hartlaub|Hartlaub]], 1855 – Sierra Leone to southwest Ghana
* ''S. e. gabonensis'' [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1883 – Ghana east to central Cameroon and north Gabon; Bioko (Gulf of Guinea)
* ''S. e. dahomeyensis'' Voelker, Tobler, Prestridge, Duijm, Groenenberg, Martin, AD, Nieman & Roselaar & Huntley, 2017 – south Benin, southeast Ghana
* ''S. e. inexpectatus'' Voelker, Tobler, Prestridge, Duijm, Groenenberg, Martin, AD, Nieman & Roselaar & Huntley, 2017 – southwest Ghana

zh.extract:
橙胸林鸲（台湾作林鴝，学名：Stiphrornis erythrothorax），是雀形目鹟科的一种鸟类，属于单型的橙胸林鸲属（学名：Stiphrornis）。
橙胸林鸲体重约15.6克，翼长约63.9毫米，喙宽度约3.5毫米，喙厚度约3.7毫米，嘴峰长约14.3毫米，跗蹠长约22.4毫米，尾长约34.3毫米。
橙胸林鸲是留鸟，栖息在森林，它们是食肉动物，主要以昆虫、蜘蛛等陆生无脊椎动物为食。
该物种有以下8个亚种:

S. e. pyrrholaemus Schmidt & Angehr, 2008
S. e. erythrothorax Hartlaub, 1855
S. e. gabonensis Sharpe, 1883
S. e. dahomeyensis Voelker et al., 2016
S. e. inexpectatus Voelker et al., 2016
S. e. xanthogaster Sharpe, 1903
S. e. sanghensis Beresford & Cracraft, 1999
S. e. rudderi Voelker et al., 2016
它们的系统发生学关系如下：
